summary: Spolsky reflects on how arsDigita, Philip Greenspun's internet consulting company, inspired Fog Creek Software through its authentic personal voice and educational mission. He traces his own discovery of the early web through Greenspun's captivating personal writing style, then analyzes what arsDigita got right (personal voice) and wrong (consulting-only model, anti-Microsoft religious bigotry). The post becomes an elegy for arsDigita after it took venture capital, grew rapidly, lost Greenspun, and replaced its distinctive voice with generic corporate marketing speak. Spolsky uses the metaphor of spring and youthful optimism to mourn the loss of authenticity that comes with institutional maturity.
key_points: A personal, human voice on the internet is what makes companies relatable and compelling — more than open source, community, or technology choices

Consulting doesn't scale the way software licensing does: consulting yields 10-20% margins while each marginal software unit sold is 100% profit, explaining why software companies are valued ~25x higher
Technology bigotry (refusing to use Microsoft products on principle rather than pragmatic grounds) is a form of religious thinking that gets in the way of doing good work
Taking venture capital forced arsDigita to grow from 5 to 250 people, which led to boring old-school management replacing the authentic voice with corporate happy-talk
The transition from scrappy authenticity to corporate maturity is a recurring tragedy in tech companies
money_quotes: What excites me was the way arsDigita had a personal voice on the Internet that made it possible to relate to it in a human way, which is what people want to do, since we're humans.
A consultant working on a project makes ten or twenty percent profit. If you license software, each marginal unit you sell is 100% profit.
I don't believe anything. Gets in the way of learning.

If you say, 'we know Unix better, so we get better results with Unix,' you're a scientist. If you say, 'we don't use any Microsoft crap, it all sucks' you're just a fanatic, and it's getting in the way of doing good work.
We don't have venture capital; we have money.
If you have a voice on your website, they will care.
key_insight: A company's authentic personal voice is its most valuable and fragile asset — venture capital, rapid growth, and professional management almost inevitably destroy it.
